point-01.caption: Placement first, tile second
point-01.situation: East 2-2, LuckyJ is dealer with a 35,000 point lead. The hand has a pair of [[3m]], a clean [[5p]][[6p]][[7p]] block, a loose souzu shape, and the important [[E]] still in hand. The dora indicator is [[N]], so [[E]] carries three jobs: round wind, dealer seat wind, and dora. The hand can become a heavy dealer hand while skipping a thin cheap route.
point-01.read: The [[W]] and [[S]] comparison is only the surface. LuckyJ aims beyond the fastest possible 1,000 point hand. With the lead and dealer turn, the hand has two jobs at once: keep enough shape to threaten a large dealer win, and avoid turning a favorable round into an unnecessary deal-in. A human looking only at tile order will ask which honor is marginally worse. LuckyJ starts with the higher-level question: what does this hand have to become for the risk to be worth taking?
point-01.whyNot: NAGA's top tile, [[S]], is a normal efficiency-style outside discard. It is reasonable. Still, choosing among side honors is a low-impact local problem compared with protecting the [[E]] plan and avoiding early speed bias. If this hand later draws central tiles, LuckyJ can still turn it into value. If the table becomes dangerous, it still has defensive shape.
point-01.copy: In your own games, start by naming the placement job before judging the discard. Leading as dealer, keep hands from turning into automatic speed races. Keep the tiles that let the hand become worth pushing, and cut the tiles with little value or defense. This is especially important when one tile, like [[E]] here, is simultaneously yaku, dora, and dealer pressure.
point-01.limit: Use this as a lead-with-value pattern. With a thin hand, low dealer pressure, or a live dangerous opponent, the correct plan can be cheap closure or an earlier fold. The [[W]] discard matters because the score job is defined first.

point-02.caption: Controlled ambiguity
point-02.situation: LuckyJ has triplet [[1p]], pair [[1s]], red [[5sr]], loose [[6m]][[8m]], [[7p]][[8p]], and three honors. The table is still early, but every player has made several discards. LuckyJ cuts [[3p]] while NAGA prefers the dragon [[C]].
point-02.read: The hand is messy and has several possible identities: toitoi-like material through [[1p]] and [[1s]], a possible open route through honors, outside-tile safety, and enough red-five value to keep playing. Cutting [[C]] would make the hand prettier in the short term, but it removes one of the few tiles that can become a yaku switch or a defensive pause button. Cutting [[3p]] admits that the pinzu side needs an immediate [[2p]] to stay central.
point-02.whyNot: NAGA's [[C]] discard is easy to understand: single dragons often leave early when they are unpaired. This hand wants more than a normal five-block riichi route. Stripping honors too quickly leaves bad shapes, a hand that may need to open, and fewer safe stops. LuckyJ keeps more identities alive and delays the commitment.
point-02.copy: When the hand is messy, ask which tile is a switch. A switch can turn the hand into yaku, raise value, allow a call, or let you stop later. If a simple tile belongs to a weak route and an honor still gives the hand a second plan, the simple tile may go first.
point-02.limit: Use this while multiple routes are live. A clean fast route or a clearly dead unsafe honor makes ambiguity sentimental.

point-03.caption: Call the tile that fixes the hand's worst problem
point-03.situation: LuckyJ is behind in East 4 with a hand full of honors and bad shapes: [[2m]][[2m]], [[1s]][[3s]][[4s]][[6s]], and pairs of [[S]] and [[P]]. Toimen discards [[P]], and LuckyJ pons, discarding [[2m]].
point-03.read: This call fixes the hand's biggest problem. The closed hand is too clumsy to trust: low speed, low riichi value, and too many tiles that become awkward if an opponent speeds up. The [[P]] pon gives the hand its yaku condition immediately. Once the hand has that yaku condition, cutting [[2m]] removes a pair with a small role in the open plan and starts turning a fake closed hand into a real open hand.
point-03.whyNot: The common mistake is to pass the first yakuhai because the hand is far from ready. That logic is backwards in this type of hand. Being far away is exactly why the yakuhai call matters: it removes the requirement to assemble a perfect closed hand. If you pass, you may spend several turns drawing around bad shapes while opponents move forward.
point-03.copy: With far-away hands, call the tile that repairs the biggest structural defect first. Yakuhai that gives the hand a yaku is often more important than completing an already decent sequence. After calling, immediately choose the discard that clarifies the route and leaves the hand able to stop.
point-03.limit: Call because the call creates a clear purpose: yaku, real speed, denial, or draw-point equity. A bad hand with no yaku after the call, low value, and no safe reserve stays bad.

point-04.caption: Open hands must still be able to stop
point-04.situation: After the first [[P]] pon, LuckyJ later pons [[S]] from toimen and discards [[W]]. The hand is now open twice, while still holding [[N]][[N]] and useful defensive material.
point-04.read: This is the difference between constructive opening and panic opening. LuckyJ is behind, so speed has value, but the second call also makes the hand shorter. Once your hand is open, you draw fewer random safe tiles and you must play more from what remains. Discarding [[W]] after the [[S]] pon says: the yaku route is now established, but the hand still needs an exit. The pair of [[N]] can be value, shape, or safety depending on how the table develops.
point-04.whyNot: Many players make the first call correctly and then make the second call badly. They see the hand getting closer and forget that every call increases future commitment. LuckyJ's open style is call with a plan for the next dangerous draw.
point-04.copy: After every open call, pause and name your remaining safe material. If the answer is 'none,' the call must be very high value or very close to winning. If the hand is still slow, leave yourself an honor, genbutsu, or a route to fold. The shorter your hand, the more expensive each safe tile becomes.
point-04.limit: Open hands that are already winning-quality can spend more safety. When the hand is still developing, safety is what lets the open hand keep playing.

point-05.caption: Cut the tile that will get worse later
point-05.situation: LuckyJ is behind in East 3 with many pair-like shapes: [[2m]][[2m]], [[2p]][[2p]], [[3s]][[3s]], [[N]][[N]], plus [[7p]][[8p]] and red [[5sr]]. Several rivers already contain terminals and honors. LuckyJ cuts [[8p]] while NAGA prefers [[1s]].
point-05.read: [[8p]] has apparent usefulness because it belongs to [[7p]][[8p]], but the hand's route is a pair-heavy value route. If the hand stays slow, [[8p]] can become exactly the kind of middle-late tile you hate releasing after opponents have shown more shape. [[1s]] is less efficient, but it is outside, often safer, and can still support a pair/chiitoi/toitoi-style hand.
point-05.whyNot: NAGA's [[1s]] discard treats the hand more like ordinary block selection. LuckyJ prices future danger more heavily. The current turn looks quiet, yet the hand is far enough away that danger can appear first. A useful-looking floater can become worse than a dead-looking outside tile.
point-05.copy: On slow hands, identify the tile whose safety will deteriorate fastest. Central suit tiles outside your main value route should often be cut before the table becomes loud. You are cleaning the hand so that, if you later choose to push, the remaining discards are less poisonous.
point-05.limit: Pre-cut middle tiles when they lack a concrete job. Dora connections, strongest blocks, and needed value seeds should stay.

point-06.caption: Build the value early
point-06.situation: South 1, LuckyJ is behind with red [[5mr]], a [[5s]][[5s]] pair, [[3p]][[4p]], and connected souzu. LuckyJ cuts isolated [[9p]] while NAGA prefers [[S]].
point-06.read: The hand has enough red-five and middle-tile material to become a real scoring hand, while still needing cleanup. The isolated [[9p]] does little for the hand's best future. [[S]] has uncertain value, but it is still a safety/yaku switch while the hand is far away. LuckyJ spends the tile that neither improves value nor protects the hand later.
point-06.whyNot: NAGA's [[S]] discard is the standard remove-isolated-honor move. LuckyJ can reject it because the [[9p]] has low real use. Keeping [[S]] keeps the hand open to value, calls, or safety depending on the next few draws.
point-06.copy: When you need points, aim beyond the smallest version of the hand. Keep the tiles that can make it worth pushing before the hand becomes narrow. Red fives, yakuhai, dora shapes, and pair-heavy routes should be planned early.
point-06.limit: Keep value plans concrete. A decorative yaku that rarely completes can still damage a good hand. LuckyJ keeps the [[S]] here because the cost is an isolated [[9p]].

point-07.caption: Tempo calls need a purpose
point-07.situation: In the same East 4 hand, LuckyJ later chis red [[5sr]] from kamicha and discards [[1s]]. The hand is already open and behind. The call is about making the open hand actually move.
point-07.read: Once you have opened around yakuhai, the hand's failure mode is becoming a slow exposed hand with weak winning and folding prospects. The red-five chi is a tempo call because it converts loose shape into a usable block and keeps the hand's value alive. Discarding [[1s]] after the call trims the least useful outside tile and keeps the hand aimed at completion.
point-07.whyNot: Players often judge calls by whether the called set looks good in isolation. A tempo call needs to change the round clock: get you close enough to matter, deny opponents free turns, or preserve a draw-point route. A call that exposes the hand while leaving the same bad shapes is just exposure.
point-07.copy: Before calling, say the purpose out loud. 'This call makes yaku and real 1-away.' 'This call takes tenpai.' 'This call preserves noten payments safely.' If you cannot finish that sentence, pass. If you can, call and immediately discard the tile that best supports the purpose.
point-07.limit: Use tempo with value, safety, or closeness. LuckyJ calls selectively because the call changes what the hand can realistically accomplish.

point-08.caption: Riichi as table control
point-08.situation: South 1, LuckyJ is above starting score with a clean closed hand: connected manzu, [[4p]][[4p]], [[3s]][[4s]][[5sr]], and a lone [[F]]. LuckyJ discards [[F]] and reaches.
point-08.read: This is a pressure conversion spot. The hand has moved past abstract shape exercise. Once [[F]] leaves, the hand is ready to present a closed threat with enough structure and value pressure that opponents must respond. Riichi here asks for table impact more than perfection. The declaration itself changes the table by taxing every opponent's future discard.
point-08.whyNot: The conservative mistake is waiting for a prettier hand when the current hand already has enough table impact. Waiting has a cost: opponents get free draws, your safe-tile count changes, and the hand can lose its current moment. LuckyJ converts when the hand is good enough and the table is still quiet.
point-08.copy: Ask whether riichi is doing work beyond adding one han. Does it force a leader to stop? Does it deny open hands? Does it make your placement target reachable? Does waiting realistically improve the hand before opponents move? If the answers favor pressure, declare.
point-08.limit: Riichi still needs a job. Against two live threats, with a low-value bad wait, or when dama preserves a better placement line, staying quiet can be stronger.

point-09.caption: A push can expire in one draw
point-09.situation: East 3, LuckyJ is behind. Toimen has called [[4p]][[5p]][[6p]] around the dora indicator [[4p]], and the table is no longer quiet. LuckyJ cuts [[E]] while NAGA prefers [[1s]].
point-09.read: This is the point where hand quality and table quality diverge. [[1s]] may look like a normal outside trim, but the NAGA danger estimate marks it as meaningfully riskier. [[E]] is much safer in this table state. LuckyJ still sees attacking futures, while keeping the current push path from starting with an unnecessary dangerous tile into an open hand.
point-09.whyNot: A common human error is to decide "I am pushing this hand" and keep pushing the same way after the table changes. The open hand, dora neighborhood, and river length all change the price. NAGA's [[1s]] may preserve a cleaner hand shape, while LuckyJ values continuing with lower immediate danger.
point-09.copy: Reprice every push after each draw and each call. Count the dangerous tiles you must pass along the whole path. If the first required discard is already uncomfortable and the hand has modest payoff, take the safe route that preserves some future chance.
point-09.limit: Use this as repricing. High-value tenpai, parent hands, and placement-critical spots can still justify the dangerous tile. Push-fold is a sequence.

point-10.caption: Late turns punish vague upside
point-10.situation: East 3, the same hand has reached the late row. Toimen is open, rivers are long, and LuckyJ is still behind. LuckyJ cuts [[N]] while NAGA prefers [[W]].
point-10.read: Late hands have less time for theory. Earlier, a tile can be kept because it may support value, safety, or a future route. With 24 tiles left, those maybes shrink. LuckyJ's [[N]] discard should be read as an exact endgame decision: which honor is the better part of the remaining push/fold plan, and which one can be released now while preserving the next discard?
point-10.whyNot: NAGA's [[W]] preference may be more efficient or mechanically cleaner. In the third row, mechanical cleanliness needs an exact objective: winning, taking safe tenpai, or folding. A discard that misses those outcomes is just a habit.
point-10.copy: In the late row, ban vague phrases like 'maybe value' or 'maybe shape.' Replace them with exact goals: safe tenpai, winning tenpai, fold, or forced push. Then choose the discard that best supports that exact goal while respecting the opponent rivers.
point-10.limit: This is one of the hardest areas to copy from LuckyJ. When NAGA, Mortal, and your own point calculation all mark the late push as bad, treat the LuckyJ move as a review prompt.

point-11.caption: Draw points are part of defense
point-11.situation: East 3, 19 tiles remain. Shimocha has two calls, including visible [[P]][[P]][[P]], and LuckyJ has a hand that may no longer be likely to win outright. LuckyJ discards [[P]] while NAGA prefers [[W]], and the hand ends in a draw with LuckyJ gaining 1,500.
point-11.read: The important idea is that defense has more than two states. In late hands, noten payments and safe tenpai are real objectives. [[P]] is heavily visible because of the pon, so releasing it can be a way to stay alive while preserving a route to draw points. Keeping [[W]] may be less about hand shape and more about how the last few turns can be navigated.
point-11.whyNot: NAGA's [[W]] discard may look natural if you only ask which tile is isolated. LuckyJ is asking what the hand can still win from the draw. If the direct win is unlikely, a safe path to tenpai can be worth more than a slightly prettier shape that exposes you to a bad final discard.
point-11.copy: When the wall is short, calculate noten payment as part of EV. If you can stay safe and keep a realistic tenpai path, do it. Look for tiles that are safe because they are visible, dead, or already accepted by the threatening hand.
point-11.limit: Draw-tenpai value only counts when the path is safe. If preserving tenpai requires throwing a live dangerous tile into a valuable opponent hand, the correct move may be full fold.

point-12.caption: A split is a study spot
point-12.situation: East 1, first discard. LuckyJ has [[1m]][[4m]][[7m]], [[1p]][[2p]][[3p]], [[3s]][[4s]], [[8s]][[9s]][[9s]], and honors [[E]][[N]]. LuckyJ discards [[N]], while NAGA prefers [[1m]].
point-12.read: This is a quiet first-discard spot. It may be tile-order preference, route preference, or a small model difference. The useful habit is to ask what each discard is trying to keep. [[N]] cleans the honor and leaves the loose manzu tiles a little longer. [[1m]] trims the isolated terminal and keeps the honor's safety or pair utility.
point-12.whyNot: A high NAGA preference gives a strong review signal, and LuckyJ's result edge gives another signal. Here, the move is early enough that many future draws can justify either plan. Use it to practice reading the two plans before chasing a perfect match rate.
point-12.copy: When reviewing, write the competing claims. What route does LuckyJ keep? What route does NAGA keep? Which claim is more important for the score and table? If the answer is unclear, mark the hand as uncertain.
point-12.limit: This is the discipline that keeps the whole project honest. Copy LuckyJ when you can explain the purchase: value, safety, speed, pressure, or placement. Unclear purchases become study material.

point-13.caption: Deny the open hand's missing yaku condition
point-13.situation: An opponent has opened in the first row with exposed tiles and an unclear yaku. LuckyJ holds a loose yaku-condition yakuhai such as [[P]], [[F]], [[C]], the round wind, or that opponent's seat wind. The local hand still has shape choices, but the honor has changed status because it can become the open hand's missing legal yaku.
point-13.read: This is sharper than ordinary early honor-cleaning. The supporting analytics compare yaku-condition yakuhai opportunities after different kinds of open hands. Against unproven-yaku opens, LuckyJ cuts those live yaku-condition tiles at a rate close to visible-yaku pressure and far earlier than against clean tanyao-shaped opens. The point is timing: the call makes the loose honor more urgent before the hand itself has become dangerous.
point-13.whyNot: The tempting line is to keep the honor because it looks safe, or to cut a suit tile that makes your own hand cleaner. That misses the timing problem. A loose yakuhai is safe only until the open hand pairs or pons it. If your own hand is too slow to punish them, waiting can turn a harmless-looking tile into the exact discard that completes their yaku.
point-13.copy: After every opponent call, name their visible yaku. If you cannot name one, mark dragons, round wind, and their seat wind as yaku-condition tiles. Keep those tiles only when they are doing real work for you: pair value, safe fold material against a bigger threat, or an immediate attacking route. Otherwise, clean them in the first row while the price is still low.
point-13.limit: Use this with live loose yakuhai against unclear open hands. Clear opponent yaku, clean tanyao shape, dead tiles, useful pairs, and your own value needs all weaken the cleanup rule.

point-14.caption: Keep the exit with the shape
point-14.situation: LuckyJ and NAGA split on a discard where NAGA wants to spend a tile that is already genbutsu or suji to an opponent river. LuckyJ cuts a different tile and keeps that defensive exit in the hand.
point-14.read: The retained tile is doing a timing job, and the target matters. A tile that is genbutsu to the dealer differs from suji to a closed child; a non-dealer open hand is a different pressure class from dealer riichi. The paired target breakdown separates kept exits from spent exits, so a small dealer-riichi kept count should be read by timing. Dealer riichi is rarer and usually later; the useful signal is whether LuckyJ cashes the safe tile now or preserves it for one more decision.
point-14.whyNot: NAGA's top discard may be mechanically cleaner because safe tiles often look redundant while the table is quiet. The hand's future danger is invisible in shanten. Spending the only genbutsu or useful suji tile too early can make the next turn force a live middle discard that the current hand never deserved to push.
point-14.copy: Keep a small defensive inventory by opponent class. Mark exact river matches first, then suji tiles from the dealer, riichi hands, and open hands. When your hand is far or only moderately valuable, avoid spending the last useful exit against the opponent most likely to tax your next discard.
point-14.limit: Rate suji modestly. Suji is weaker than genbutsu, weaker against unusual waits, and often irrelevant against a quiet player. Copy LuckyJ here when the retained tile is safe against the player who matters and the hand still needs a future off-ramp.

point-15.caption: Spend stale safety when the target changes
point-15.situation: LuckyJ discards a tile that is already genbutsu or suji while NAGA prefers to keep that safety and cut shape. The key question is whether the safe tile still protects against the player who matters now.
point-15.read: This gives a narrow correction to simple safe-tile hoarding. The broad pattern appeared 4,057 times with a better severe-disagreement profile than ordinary mismatches, but Mortal backed LuckyJ in only 3 of 10 sampled cases. Treat it as a target check: the safe tile is valuable only if it answers the live riichi, open hand, or next likely danger point.
point-15.whyNot: NAGA's line may keep the comforting safe tile because it costs little right now. The trap is that a safe tile against the wrong player can make the hand miss its real route. If the hand is close and another exit remains, spending stale safety can be more disciplined than preserving it.
point-15.copy: Before keeping a safe tile, name its target. If the target is quiet, no longer the danger, or covered by another exit, compare the shape cost honestly. Spend the safe tile when keeping it damages the hand more than it protects the next decision.
point-15.limit: Protect your last real exit. Keep the defensive resource when the safe tile covers the live threat, the hand is still far, or the next draw would leave no safe discard.

point-16.caption: Edge cuts can keep the actual winning route
point-16.situation: LuckyJ cuts terminal or outside material while NAGA keeps it and cuts a middle tile. The hand is usually in the middle or late row, where vague future value has mostly expired.
point-16.read: The outside tile can be a shape discard as well as a safety discard. The supporting examples repeatedly show late 1s/2s, 1p/3p, and 9m/6m choices where the inside tile is the real connector and the outside tile is the cost of staying live.
point-16.whyNot: It is tempting to call the terminal safer and therefore defensive. That misses the hand route. If the middle tile accepts the winning draw or preserves tenpai, the terminal cut may be the attacking line with lower friction.
point-16.copy: When an outside cut looks strange, ask what the retained inside tile does next. If it connects the hand to tenpai, accepts a high-value draw, or avoids forcing a worse live tile later, the cut can be copied.
point-16.limit: Cut outside tiles only after checking yaku, dora support, pair value, and target-specific safety. The point applies when the outside tile has a weaker job than the inside route it is replacing.

point-17.caption: A safe tile needs a target
point-17.situation: LuckyJ keeps a genbutsu or suji tile that NAGA wants to cut, but the important detail is who that tile is safe against and whether that opponent is active.
point-17.read: The aggregate warning is strong: generic kept-genbutsu/suji mismatches were worse than baseline, while threat-specific exit preservation was much better. The concept is coverage: a tile is good when it covers the opponent who can punish the next discard.
point-17.whyNot: Humans over-label tiles as simply safe. Genbutsu to shimocha gives no protection against toimen's riichi. Suji to a quiet child gives little answer to an open dealer. If the kept tile misses the live danger, NAGA's shape cut may be right.
point-17.copy: For every safe tile you keep, write the full sentence: "safe against X if Y happens." If the sentence names a live riichi, open hand, or dealer pressure, the tile has a job. Vague targets should be repriced as ordinary clutter.
point-17.limit: Target naming can drift into over-defense. High-value tenpai, parent pressure, and weak threats can still justify spending the safe tile.

point-18.caption: Honor review starts with a role label
point-18.situation: LuckyJ cuts an honor while NAGA prefers a shape tile. The honor might be loose cleanup, opponent yaku denial, a dead tile, or a stale safety tile.
point-18.read: The loose-honor cleanup bucket was statistically strong: 2,348 cases with a 20.9% severe-disagreement rate and safer average danger. Mortal mostly sided with NAGA in the sampled high-gap cases, so the reliable lesson is to identify which honor role is actually present.
point-18.whyNot: A live dragon pair, a lone wind needed by an open opponent, and a double-visible dead honor are different review objects. Treating all of them as honors hides the real decision.
point-18.copy: Label the honor before judging the discard: self yakuhai, opponent yaku condition, value seed, dead tile, genbutsu exit, or ordinary loose honor. Then apply the rule for that label.
point-18.limit: Keep the honor when it is your value, your only exit against the live threat, or part of a real route such as honitsu or chiitoi. Clean it when its main job belongs to someone else.

point-19.caption: Leader safety still has to calculate
point-19.situation: LuckyJ is leading and chooses the lower-risk line. The lead changes the hand's job, while each passive discard still needs a concrete reason.
point-19.read: The leader-low-risk bucket had 5,153 mismatches and was only mildly better than baseline. That makes it a qualifier for other points. A lead reduces the value needed to end the hand, while the discard still must avoid real danger or preserve the next safe decision.
point-19.whyNot: The lazy interpretation is 'I am leading, so I can play safe.' LuckyJ's better leader decisions are more specific: reduce unnecessary danger while keeping a workable hand or a clean fold path.
point-19.copy: When leading, compare the discard against two jobs: end the hand cheaply if possible, and avoid creating a future forced push. Choose the tile that does both.
point-19.limit: Preserve useful shape when it still ends the hand or protects the next turn. A safe-looking discard that misses live danger or kills the only winning route is probably too passive.
